The added TemplateData/unicode handling is also broken by Macro.__call__ returning TemplateData(u''.join(...)) while do_escape now always unicode()-converts inputs, which strips the __html__ shortcut for already-safe values unless the new escape helper is used everywhere. The optimizer change also introduces a bug by marking assigned names undefined in contexts where they are merely reassigned, which can prevent legitimate constant propagation and alter loop/macro optimization semantics.

The change consistently replaces identity checks on token type strings with value equality checks, which is the correct fix for non-interned strings such as on Jython. The small defaults.py __all__ change is also benign and improves compatibility by avoiding iterator behavior differences. No functional defect is introduced.

The new async select test changes the expected behavior for `select` on a mixed list. The input now includes `0`, but the assertion still expects the filtered output to start at `0` (`'0|1|2|3|4|5'`). This contradicts the existing earlier test pattern for boolean selection, where `0` is falsy and should be excluded by `select` without a test name argument. The test as written will fail and makes the change defective.

The new absolute-symlink branch is incorrect. It computes `stripped_rv_drive` but never uses it, and then reconstructs the path with `os.path.join(rv_drive, rv_path)`, which preserves the Win32 extended-length prefix instead of stripping it. On Windows absolute symlinks will still resolve to prefixed paths, so the intended fix does not work. Depending on the exact prefix form, this can also produce an invalid path if `os.path.join` treats the drive component specially.

Removing __delitem__ breaks the intended exported-variable tracking for template contexts. The class comment and surrounding logic indicate that deleting a key should also remove it from exported so includes/get_exported no longer expose it. Without this override, deleted keys remain in exported, and get_exported() will later try to read missing keys, causing errors or stale exports.

The change is consistent with the context manager implementation: on non-Windows platforms raw_terminal() yields a file descriptor, and getchar() already uses os.read(fd, ...), so binding the yielded value to fd is required for correctness. This is a minimal fix with no apparent behavioral regression.

The refactor replaces get_next() with functools.partial(next, ...) and moves the buffered generator logic into a method. Behavior appears equivalent on Python 3, which is the only branch affected here since get_next() was removed from the non-PY2 path. The buffering loop condition change from < size to <= size is offset by the new structure and still yields after collecting the intended number of non-empty chunks; no clear functional regression is introduced from the diff shown.

The condition is inverted: previously `publish()` was only called when the last CLI argument was `publish`, but the new code calls `publish()` for every invocation except `publish`. This makes normal setup commands like `sdist`, `install`, or `--help` immediately recurse into `python setup.py sdist upload`, breaking all standard packaging workflows and causing unintended uploads.

The change only switches the test to use the pytest httpbin fixture while preserving the same behavior and assertion. It makes the test less brittle by avoiding a hardcoded external host, and there is no apparent functional defect introduced.

The change in `_split_what` replaces `isinstance(cls, type)` with `isinstance(type, cls)`, which reverses the arguments and changes the semantics completely. For normal input like a class object, this will raise `TypeError` because `isinstance()` expects the second argument to be a type or tuple of types, not the class being tested. This breaks `include()` and `exclude()` whenever type filters are used.

verdict.class_guess: test regression / invalid acceptance of mixed-case boolean strings
verdict.rationale: The new test only checks lowercase values and their `.title()` variants. For boolean strings like `false`, `no`, and `off`, `.title()` produces `False`, `No`, and `Off`, but `BoolParamType.convert` lowercases input and will accept these mixed-case forms. For the uppercase `TRUE`, `YES`, etc., `.title()` also works. However the implementation change itself does not actually alter accepted inputs beyond using a set for the false values, so the likely defect is in the test: it assumes title-cased variants are a meaningful check, and it no longer verifies the original default-handling behavior. More importantly, the error message changed to include quotes and the word 'value', which may break existing expectations elsewhere. The main merge risk here is the test logic, not the conversion itself.

Changing CodeGenerator.signature() default from have_comma=True to False breaks callers that rely on the default comma separation. In particular, visit_Filter() and visit_Test() call signature() without passing have_comma, so their generated call arguments will now be emitted without a leading comma after the first positional argument, producing invalid Python like `context.tests['x'](valuearg=...)` instead of `context.tests['x'](value, arg=...)`. The unused import removal is fine, but this default change introduces a functional regression.

The refactor changes short-help truncation behavior and appears to break the guarantee that the returned string never exceeds max_length. In the truncation path, the code first allows a word to push total_length past max_length, then only removes words while i > 0 and returns ' '.join(words[:i]) + '...'. This can leave a result longer than max_length in edge cases, especially when the first word itself is too long or when removing the last kept word is necessary but i reaches 0. The new tests cover some cases, but not these boundary conditions.

The first change is a documentation typo fix only. The second change in Attribute.__init__ tightens argument validation from '>' to '>=' so passing exactly the full number of declared slots now correctly raises 'Too many arguments.' Since __slots__ has 8 entries and __init__ handles 'convert' as optional, the previous condition allowed constructing Attribute with all 8 keywords, which is erroneous because one of them would exceed the intended parameter set when combined with the optional default handling. The new check is consistent with the fixed slot count and does not introduce a defect.

The change only replaces literal non-ASCII characters with equivalent Unicode escape sequences in test data. This preserves behavior while improving source encoding robustness and consistency. No functional defect is introduced.

The .hgtags removal matches the PR description (untagging 1.2 for retagging) and does not affect runtime behavior. In parser.py, the lineno fixes improve error reporting by using the current stream line, and changing parse_tuple_expression(simplified=True) to use parse_expression in the simplified path makes the branch consistent with the non-simplified path and is not obviously incorrect. I do not see a functional defect introduced by this diff.

The change correctly updates Choice shell completion to honor case-insensitive matching when case_sensitive=False, while preserving existing case-sensitive behavior. The added test covers both modes and the implementation appears consistent with Choice.convert semantics. No obvious defect is introduced.

This removes the only code path that honored the documented `accept_gzip` setting, but `defaults.py` still documents `accept_gzip` as a supported configuration option. Existing callers that rely on setting `config['accept_gzip']=True` to force `Accept-Encoding: gzip` will stop working, changing request headers and potentially response handling behavior. The config key is also left dangling in docs/comments, indicating an incomplete removal rather than a clean deprecation.

The tuple subclass used for __attrs_attrs__ is now explicitly slotted, which is safe and consistent with its intended immutable/tuple-like behavior. The helper _transform_attrs had its parameters reordered, and the only internal call site in attributes() was updated accordingly. No other references are shown, and the changed call still passes the same arguments in the new order. This appears to be a harmless refactor plus a memory optimization.

This change makes `requests.packages.chardet` and `requests.packages.urllib3` resolve to system-installed packages when the vendored copies are unavailable. That can mask missing vendored dependencies but also changes import identity and behavior: code expecting the bundled package layout may now get a different installed version, which can break compatibility in Debian environments or when only one of the dependencies is present. The aliasing via `sys.modules` also only happens after the fallback import succeeds, so any code importing submodules during package initialization may observe inconsistent module paths.
The patch only rewords a comment in PreparedRequest.prepare_body without changing executable code or behavior. No defect is introduced.

The change keeps the copy on the same deque and avoids replacing it, which preserves the already-bound queue method aliases from __init__/_postinit__. Since the copy creates a fresh deque and then extends it, there is no shared third queue and no need to rebind methods. The adjusted test reflects the intended behavior that the copy remains functional after mutation.

The new package auto-detection changes from using the calling module's __name__ to __package__, with a special-case only for __package__ == '__main__'. In the common `python -m package.module` case, `__package__` is typically `package`, so this works. However, for direct script execution or modules where `__package__` is empty/None, the fallback now leaves package_name unset instead of deriving it from `__name__` as before, causing `version_option()` to fail to auto-detect versions in cases that previously worked. This is a regression in existing behavior beyond the intended `python -m package` support.

verdict.class_guess: regression in sandboxed string.format handling for positional auto-numbered fields / escaped formatting
verdict.rationale: Removing _MagicFormatMapping changes how SandboxedEnvironment.format_string handles str.format and format_map. The old wrapper provided special handling for empty field names ({}) by mapping them to successive positional args, which is necessary for Python's formatter behavior in this sandbox path. After the change, formatter.vformat receives the raw args tuple, and the only new logic is a _used counter in SandboxedFormatterMixin, which does not implement the needed mapping for empty braces. The new test only covers simple {} cases, but there is no corresponding support for the escaped formatter path beyond the changed formatter class, so this risks breaking formatting with automatic field numbering and potentially format_map/escape formatter behavior in sandboxed templates.

The change is a straightforward internal rename/move of the bash completion module and the only in-repo import site was updated accordingly. No behavioral logic changed, and the new module name follows the intended internal-module convention.

This is a mechanical rename of the internal version module and corresponding import/type stub/test/tox references. The public API still exposes VersionInfo from attr.__init__, and the renamed files are consistently updated. The test tweak only removes a tuple case that was evidently redundant/unwanted for this suite. No functional defect is apparent from the diff.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect handling of optional weakref_slot parameter in simple_classes
verdict.rationale: The refactor changes `weakref_slot` handling from respecting the caller-provided parameter to effectively always drawing a boolean and then using the drawn value whenever `weakref_slot` is not None. This is a logic inversion relative to the existing behavior and the docstring: `weakref_slot=False` no longer guarantees `weakref_slot=False` because the expression `weakref_flag if weakref_slot is not None else weakref_slot` uses the random drawn flag for both True and False, only preserving None. The other two flags correctly use the passed value when not None, but `weakref_slot` does not, which will make tests/strategy generation incorrect for callers explicitly requesting weakref_slot=False.

The package version is updated to 21.1.0.dev0, but __version_info__ is derived by stripping '.dev0' before parsing. This makes __version_info__ report a non-development release (21.1.0) while __version__ still says dev0, creating an inconsistency that can break code/tests relying on matching version metadata.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect handling of weakref_slot override in simple_classes strategy
verdict.rationale: The refactor changed `weakref_slot` from being conditionally drawn only when the argument is `None` to always drawing a boolean and always passing that drawn value to `make_class`. This means callers can no longer force `weakref_slot=True` or `False`; the provided argument is ignored. The docstring explicitly says the same override behavior should apply to `weakref_slot` as to `slots` and `frozen`, so this is a functional regression.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect iterator length accounting after partial consumption
verdict.rationale: The new length calculation only measures the remaining items in self._iterator plus _iterations_done_count. But self._iterations_done_count is incremented in _safe_next() when the current item is prefetched, so after rendering the first item and then accessing loop.length, the count does not include the current iteration item. For a generator of N items, loop.length becomes N-1 at the first iteration (and similarly revindex/revindex0 are off by one), which is exactly the defect this change is intended to fix. The old code added index0 + 2 to account for the current item and already-consumed prefix; this replacement drops that offset.

__version__ was bumped to 21.4.0.dev0, but __version_info__ is still parsed from the old string 21.3.0. This leaves the public version metadata inconsistent, so code using __version_info__ will report the wrong release cycle/dev status and may misbehave in version checks.

The new stub introduces invalid or broken type variable declarations. `_I` is bounded by `Iterable[_T]` while `_T` is unconstrained, and `_M` is declared as `TypeVar("_V", bound=Mapping[_K, _V])`, which reuses `_V` as both the TypeVar name and the bound type variable name and also references `_K`/`_V` before they are properly established for the bound. This is likely to fail type-checking or produce incorrect inference, defeating the goal of fixing mypy behavior. The validator signatures also change the return type from item-type validators to container-type validators, which may be incompatible with existing uses beyond the intended fix.

The refactor changed completion ordering/selection semantics. Previously, when an incomplete argument was found, user autocompletions were added and then subcommand/chained-command completions could still be appended if the argument was not required. Now `get_choices()` returns immediately for any incomplete option value, and for an incomplete option it no longer has any path to subcommand completions. More importantly, on a required argument it returns only user completions, but on a non-required argument it breaks after the first incomplete argument and then adds subcommand completions; this differs from the old logic that could continue scanning for other completion possibilities only when appropriate. The early-return restructuring also makes option-value completion return a bare list directly, which is fine, but the behavior change can suppress valid completions in cases where chained subcommands should still be offered after non-required arguments or when multiple user-completion sources are expected.

The new leave_frame logic only skips VAR_LOAD_PARAMETER loads, but it now suppresses the final 'undefs = missing' assignment for all other load types when with_python_scope=True. In visit_Template this is enabled for the root frame, and root-level code still needs all unresolved symbols to be initialized to missing before later generated expressions run. More importantly, enter_frame still emits individual assignments for RESOLVE/ALIAS and collects UNDEFINED names into a batch missing assignment, but the changed leave_frame no longer performs any cleanup for python-scope frames, which can leave stale names bound across generated Python scopes and alter variable resolution semantics. The optimization also changes root template code generation behavior in a way that can break references expected to be missing.

The change only narrows the older __code__-introspection sanity check to the pre-3.8 fallback path. On CPython 3.8+, the implementation now uses direct cell_contents assignment, so the moved validation is no longer needed and removing it avoids spurious failures in environments where __code__ inspection is unreliable. The fallback path still retains the original verification, so behavior on older interpreters is preserved.

This is a documentation-only markup fix in a docstring, replacing unsupported MyST link syntax with plain inline code. It does not change runtime behavior or API semantics, and the updated text remains accurate.

This is a documentation/newsfragment addition plus a typing-example adjustment to reflect the new TypeGuard behavior of attrs.has(). The example change is consistent with the intended typing improvement and does not introduce a functional defect.

This change only adds a regression test covering slicing on an attribute access in async templates. It does not modify production code, and the test itself is consistent with the surrounding async for-loop tests. No defect is introduced by the patch.

This test is intended to track imports performed by click, but the tracking hook only records imports when globals['__name__'] starts with 'click' and level == 0. That misses many imports triggered during click's import process because once click imports its submodules, __name__ in the importer context may not start with 'click' as expected, and the level check excludes package-relative imports. As a result the test can produce false negatives or be inconsistent across Python versions. Additionally, the test only allows a hardcoded subset of stdlib modules, which can break legitimately when click's import dependencies change or differ by platform/version. This makes the new test brittle and likely to fail spuriously.

The change only adds tests for BOM handling and updates an expected value in an existing UTF-8 BOM test. No production code is changed, and the revised test expectation in guess_json_utf is consistent with the documented behavior of preserving 'utf-8-sig' when a BOM is present. No defect is introduced by this patch.

The change only stops emitting a DeprecationWarning for __version__ while keeping the existing metadata lookup behavior unchanged. The updated test correctly asserts the value and absence of warnings. No functional regression is apparent from the diff.

The tempfile pager now always closes the mkstemp file descriptor in the finally block, but the descriptor was already closed earlier on non-Windows platforms via the Windows-only branch removal. In the current code, the fd is never closed before os.close(fd) in all cases, which is fine for the pager path, but the changed logic makes the cleanup unconditional while the fd is still open only until the pager command returns. The bigger issue is the open_url CYGWIN branch: replacing url.replace('"', '') with _unquote_file(url) changes behavior for non-file URLs and may preserve quotes that the previous code intentionally stripped, potentially breaking cygstart invocation when URLs contain embedded quotes. This can cause command invocation failures on Cygwin for inputs that previously worked.
The change only updates the docstring for do_sum, adding clearer explanation and an example. No executable code or behavior changed, so it should not introduce a defect.

The rewritten test no longer validates the original behavior correctly and appears to have an incorrect expectation flow. In test_exception, after configuring MockMemcached.get/set to raise and calling dump/load with ignore_memcache_errors=True, the test switches ignore_memcache_errors to False and only asserts that dump_bytecode raises. It never asserts the corresponding load_bytecode failure path that existed before. More importantly, the test uses a single mock instance with stateful class attributes and does not reset the stored code/value between operations, so it can mask regressions or pass for the wrong reason. This weakens coverage and may let bytecode-cache error handling defects slip through.
The diff only corrects a documentation typo in the Environment.__init__ docstring ('based comments' -> 'comments') and does not change runtime behavior or API semantics.

The change only adds a Sphinx linkcheck ignore regex for GitHub issue/pull URLs to avoid rate limiting during documentation link checks. This is a reasonable configuration-only change and does not appear to introduce a functional defect.

verdict.class_guess: functional regression in async map filter
verdict.rationale: The async map implementation now swaps the unpacking order from prepare_map to `func, seq`, but this is very likely incorrect. In Jinja2, `prepare_map` returns the mapped sequence first and the mapper function second (as used by the synchronous `do_map` implementation). Reversing them will either make `seq` a callable or `func` an iterable, breaking async map in general. The new await logic itself is intended, but it is applied to the wrong values because of the swapped assignment.

The new module appears internally consistent and implements the documented introspection helpers without an obvious correctness issue. It correctly subclasses the code generator to collect undeclared identifiers and iterates referenced template nodes, yielding constants or None for dynamic templates as intended. No defect is evident from the added file alone.

The change fixes a real issue in i18n pluralization with call expressions by assigning the callable result to a temporary variable before using it as the plural count, and the comment extraction tweak corrects same-line comment association. The updated tests reflect the intended behavior. I do not see a new defect introduced by this patch.

The change fixes an incorrect conditional in create_cache so that cache_size values of 0 and negative values both disable caching consistently. The docstring typo fix is harmless. No defect introduced.

The new test defines the response hook as a local nested function (`print_url`). Local functions are not pickleable with the standard `pickle` module, so `pickle.dumps(p)` is likely to fail when the PreparedRequest contains this hook. This makes the added test invalid and it does not actually verify pickleability with hooks; it will fail before the send step.

The change correctly fixes volatile eval-context scoping by marking frame.eval_ctx.volatile instead of frame.volatile, and the output-generation path now closes the added conditional expression parentheses correctly. The added test exercises the intended behavior, and the relaxed extension test reflects a non-deterministic representation detail rather than a regression. No obvious functional defect is introduced.

The change only adds 'colorama' to the whitelist of allowed imports in the import-lightness test. This is consistent with a new optional dependency being imported during click's import path and does not by itself introduce a defect.

This change appears to break redirect behavior by moving redirect resolution out of Session.request into Session.send and then passing non-send arguments ('allow_redirects' and 'req') through send_kwargs. However, Session.send now calls adapter.send(request, **kwargs) with kwargs still containing 'req' and 'allow_redirects' if they are not popped in all call paths, which adapters do not accept and would raise TypeError. More importantly, redirect history is now only assembled in send, but Session.request no longer resolves redirects at all, so any other code path relying on request() behavior may regress. The elapsed test fix is harmless, but the redirect refactor is risky and likely introduces a defect in request dispatch/signature compatibility.

The changelog update is non-functional. The version bump to 21.5.0.dev0 with __version_info__ derived from dev1 is consistent with a new development cycle, and nothing in the diff appears to break runtime behavior. The added changelog guidance is documentation-only.
This change only bumps the package version from 0.4 to 0.5 in setup.py. No functional code paths or packaging metadata beyond the version number are altered, so it does not introduce a defect.

The change is limited to a documentation/example formatting update and a minor non-functional tweak in the progress bar filter condition. The label shortening only affects displayed text, and changing '>' to '>=' alters the sample's random filtering rate slightly but does not introduce a defect in the example code.

The refactor preserves behavior in the editor workflow: text normalization still occurs only for non-bytes input, file handles are now safely managed with context managers, and `isinstance` is a safer replacement for `type(...) in [...]` while correctly treating `bytearray` as binary input. Changing `env=None` to `os.environ` in the no-custom-env case is also equivalent for `subprocess.Popen` here. No clear defect introduced.

The new test contains a typo in the truthy value list: `'t,' '1'` concatenates into `'t,1'`, so the loop will test `'t,1'` instead of `'t'` and `'1'`. This makes the intended boolean conversion coverage incorrect and may cause the test to fail or miss the `'t'`/`'1'` cases.

The change is narrowly targeted: FrameIdentifierVisitor.visit_CallBlock now excludes call block arguments from outer-scope identifier tracking, which matches the bug description about an argument name colliding with an inner-frame local. The added regression test exercises the reported scenario and passes the argument through caller(user) correctly. No obvious regression is introduced by ignoring args here, since args are handled as parameters in function_scoping/macro_body and should not be treated as outer-scope references.

The change preserves intended behavior while avoiding unnecessary empty list creation. Returning lists from get_user_autocompletions is compatible with the existing extend() usage in get_choices, and the nargs >= 1 adjustment correctly treats fixed-arity arguments with one required value as potentially incomplete when only partially filled. No regressions are apparent from this diff.

The new cert gating matches the intent of not attaching client certificates when verify is false, but the surrounding exception handling introduces a serious regression: catching MaxRetryError and doing `pass` leaves `r` undefined when retries are exhausted or connection setup fails, so `_build_response(r)` can raise an UnboundLocalError instead of propagating a connection failure or safe-mode response. This changes a handled network error into an internal crash. Additionally, using `len(self.cert) == 2` instead of `type(self.cert) is tuple` is a broader behavior change, but the primary defect is the swallowed MaxRetryError.

Changing the split logic from value.split(",") to re.split(",\ *<", value) breaks parsing of standard Link header formats and can drop/merge entries incorrectly. The new regex only splits when a comma is followed by optional spaces and a literal '<', so headers like '<a>; rel=one, <b>; rel=two' may not be split as intended once the leading '<' is consumed, and any comma-separated link value not matching that exact pattern will be parsed as a single malformed link. This is a behavior regression in a public utility function.

The change only updates the changelog with release notes for 17.4.0 and bumps the package version from 17.4.0.dev0 to 17.4.0. No functional code paths are modified, and the version string matches the release notes. This appears acceptable.

The change only skips a platform-specific test on Windows and simplifies the test invocation by removing resolve_path=True, which is not needed for the assertion being made. The reordered test arguments are harmless. No defect is introduced by this diff.
No diff or file changes were provided, so there is no visible code change to evaluate for defects. Based on the available information, I cannot identify a introduced issue.

This change only replaces unittest's assertIsNone with a plain assert checking the same condition. It does not alter test behavior or production code, and in this test file both assertion styles are acceptable.
The change only removes an unused import (`bytes`) from `requests.compat`. The file still uses `str` from compat for Python 2/3 compatibility, and no behavior is altered.

The new verify support is broken in two ways. First, `verify = verify or self.verify` prevents explicitly disabling verification with `verify=False` on a request when the session default is truthy, because `False or self.verify` resolves to the session value. Second, the session-level `verify` attribute is added to `Session.__init__` but is not included in `__attrs__`, so it is never merged into request args the way other session defaults are. As a result, the new feature does not reliably propagate or override verification settings.

verdict.class_guess: Path symlink resolution regression on non-Windows/absolute symlinks
verdict.rationale: The new logic changes symlink handling so that absolute symlinks are no longer joined to dir_. For absolute links on POSIX, os.readlink() returns an absolute path and the prior os.path.join(dir_, rv) correctly preserved it. The replacement code strips the drive component and then joins the remainder, which can turn an absolute path into a relative one (e.g. '/tmp/target' becomes 'tmp/target'), causing os.stat/readability checks to operate on the wrong path or fail. The condition `rv.endswith(os.path.sep)` is also not a reliable indicator of an absolute link target. This appears to break existing resolve_path behavior beyond the intended Windows prefix stripping.

The new length calculation increments `_iterations_done_count` when `_safe_next()` prefetches `_after`, but `loop.length` is defined as total items remaining plus items already consumed. At initialization `_after` already advances one element, so counting it as a completed iteration makes `length` off by one for generator-backed loops before the first rendered item and can miscompute `revindex0`/`revindex` semantics. The prior code used `index0 + 2` to compensate for this prefetch behavior; replacing it with the raw `_iterations_done_count` loses that adjustment.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect test file execution guard and flaky/external cookie test
verdict.rationale: The final guard was inverted from `if __name__ == '__main__'` to `if __name__ != '__main__'`, which prevents the test suite from running when the file is executed directly and instead runs it on import. Also, the new cookie test depends on an external website and assumes a specific cookie name/value round-trip from that service; this makes the test brittle and likely to fail for reasons unrelated to the code under test.

verdict.class_guess: Behavioral regression in assoc() unknown-attribute handling
verdict.rationale: The change alters `assoc()` so unknown keyword changes are silently ignored (`continue`) instead of raising `AttrsAttributeNotFoundError`. That is a user-visible behavior regression and breaks existing API contract documented in the function docstring and covered by tests. The test update also removes coverage for this error path, so the defect would slip through. Additionally, the implementation now uses `getattr(attrs, k, None)` on the attribute tuple, which is not equivalent to the prior explicit missing-attribute check and is paired with the silent-drop behavior.
The change only removes commented-out dead code and debug print comments. It does not alter runtime behavior or interfaces, so no defect is introduced.

The change is a docstring cleanup plus a test restoration. The only functional code change is warning stacklevel=1 in just_warn(), which makes the warning point to the helper itself rather than the caller, but this path is only used as a fallback on interpreters lacking code-object support and does not affect behavior or correctness. The added test broadens coverage for 2-arg super() in property definitions. No defect is introduced.

The change consistently updates attribute-based filters to treat only None as the absence of a default, allowing falsey values like False, 0, '' or [] to be used intentionally. The updated tests cover representative falsey defaults, and the removed exception in map's argument parsing preserves the existing error behavior for missing filter arguments via the subsequent call path.

The replacement test no longer verifies the original behavior of handling invalid UTF-8 bytes input. It changes the input from bytes to a Unicode string, which can mask regressions in the bytes-decoding path that `format_filename` is meant to exercise. Additionally, the extra added assertion duplicates the existing `foo.txt` case, so the test now has less meaningful coverage than before.

The change correctly updates the result callback chaining wrapper to forward *args/**kwargs through to both the existing and new callbacks, which matches the documented behavior of passing through main-callback parameters. The new wrapper preserves chaining semantics and should be backward compatible for existing single-argument result callbacks because extra args/kwargs will be empty in that case.

The change removes the wheel universal flag as intended for a Python 3.6+ project and moves README long description handling into setup.cfg. The setup.py version parsing regex is made slightly more permissive, but it still correctly extracts the version from the same source file. No functional defect is apparent from the diff.

The new helper `choose_async()` is implemented with reversed semantics: it returns `sync_value` when `environment.is_async` is true, and `async_value` otherwise. This inverts several generated constructs. For example, `func()` will emit `def` in async mode and `async def` in sync mode, which is invalid for async templates and can break compilation/runtime. Similar inversions affect `await` prefixes and `async for`/`for` selection across imports, blocks, and loops.

The call to do_it was changed from do_it(cls, auto_attribs) to do_it(auto_attribs, cls), but do_it is defined as do_it(cls, auto_attribs) and passes its first argument as maybe_cls and second as auto_attribs into attrs(). This reverses the class and auto_attribs values, so when auto_attribs is explicitly set the decorator will attempt to decorate a boolean as the class and pass the class object as auto_attribs, breaking class creation.

The change correctly extends PackageLoader to handle PEP 451 specs where submodule_search_locations is None by falling back to the package module’s parent directory via spec.origin. This preserves existing behavior for regular packages and namespace packages, and the added tests cover source loading and listing for a single-module package. No defect is apparent from the diff.

The change cleanly removes the deprecated keyword form of attrs.evolve(inst=...) and updates tests/documentation accordingly. The new implementation preserves the intended positional-only behavior, and the added/adjusted tests cover missing and excessive positional arguments. I did not find a functional defect introduced by this change.

The functional change in this diff is limited to correcting the gt docstring from operator.ge to operator.gt, which is a documentation fix only. The unrelated change in disabled() restores the prior validator state instead of always enabling validators on exit; this is an improvement and prevents a potential bug, not a defect introduced by this patch.

verdict.class_guess: cache key collision / stale cache bug
verdict.rationale: Using the template name whenever loader.get_source() returns a falsey cache_key changes cache semantics for any non-file-backed loader that intentionally returns an empty string or other falsey-but-valid key to mean "shared source identity". Distinct templates can now collide in the environment cache under the same name-based key, causing wrong templates to be returned and stale bytecode/source reuse across different resources. The previous code used the loader-provided key verbatim, which preserved loader-specific caching behavior.

This change only adds unit tests for existing hooks behavior. The tests appear consistent with the expected dispatch_hook/default_hooks semantics and do not modify production code, so no defect is introduced by the patch itself.

The change only updates the inline comment/documentation for Session.max_redirects to clarify that exceeding the limit raises TooManyRedirects. No executable code or behavior changed, so there is no functional defect introduced.

The change is consistent with the stated goal: using a copied session cookie jar and merging explicit request cookies so request cookies take precedence, and using update() to persist response cookies. Implementing RequestsCookieJar.copy() via update(self) matches the existing update semantics and preserves cookie contents. I do not see an introduced functional defect in the patched paths.

The change only removes a dead/duplicate test method from tests/test_make.py. No production code is altered, and the remaining test suite still covers the behavior mentioned by the removed test (validator=None treated the same as an empty validator in TestValidate.test_multiple_empty).

The change updates Ruff and adjusts a few tests to use identity comparisons where the resolved objects are expected to be the same singleton objects (e.g. builtins, typing aliases, object). The one behavioral test change in test_next_gen.py replaces an exception-raising branch with pass, but the surrounding assertions are on the exception object captured by pytest and this test is specifically about exception context handling, so the intended coverage remains. No functional defect is introduced by the dependency bump or the test updates.
No diff or file contents were provided, so there is no evidence of a defect introduced by this change. Based on the available information, the change cannot be assessed as faulty.

The documentation wording change is harmless, and removing the Python <3.7 skip from the Pyright test is consistent with the project’s current type-annotation support. The test logic itself still only depends on pyright being installed, and the remaining assertions are unchanged.

verdict.class_guess: Encoding/Windows newline handling regression in Editor.edit
verdict.rationale: The refactor changed Windows text-mode handling incorrectly. Previously, when editing text on Windows, the code converted newlines from LF to CRLF before UTF-8-SIG encoding. The new code uses `text.replace("\n", "\n")`, which is a no-op, so files are written with LF-only line endings. Many Windows editors (including Notepad) expect CRLF and may display the file incorrectly or preserve unexpected formatting, breaking the intended behavior of the editor flow.

The change flips the Python-version skip check from `> 2` to `>= 2`, which means the test will now be skipped on all Python 2.x versions as well as Python 3.x. The test body uses old-style classes and is intended to run on Python 2 only, so this condition is actually correct relative to the comment; however, compared to the existing behavior, it fixes the skip logic rather than introducing a defect. The only real defect in the diff is the removed `print()`, which is harmless. Because the changed skip condition alters test execution unexpectedly if the project still supports Python 2, this should be reviewed carefully.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect argument ordering / variable name swap causing wrong containment and sandbox attribute lookup behavior
verdict.rationale: The change appears to introduce two regressions. In `Context.__contains__`, the method signature was swapped to `def __contains__(name, self)`, which reverses the expected `self, name` ordering; calls like `key in context` will now treat the context object as the lookup key and the string name as the context, breaking containment checks and likely raising attribute errors. In `SandboxedEnvironment.getattr`, the fallback from `obj[argument]` to `obj[attribute]` and the final undefined construction using `name=attribute` instead of `argument` changes behavior and can break lookups for non-string arguments or produce misleading undefined objects for item access.

The compiler change broadens the conditional extend fix from top-level frames to any frame outside a block, which aligns with the intended semantics for conditional extending and prevents partial loop execution in visible output. The i18n change safely falls back from ugettext/ungettext to gettext/ngettext when those legacy methods are absent, improving compatibility with custom translation objects without altering behavior for standard gettext installs. No clear regression is introduced by the diff.

The pickle test now calls `pickle.dumps(protocol, cache)` instead of `pickle.dumps(cache, protocol)`. This passes the protocol integer as the object to serialize and the cache as the protocol argument, which is invalid and will fail or serialize the wrong object. The test no longer verifies LRUCache pickling and will not work as intended.

The env restoration bug is fixed by capturing os.environ.get(key) instead of os.environ.get(value), and the added test exercises that behavior. The default color=True change is not a defect because invoke() still explicitly passes its color argument (defaulting to False), so existing behavior is preserved for callers of invoke() while the isolation context itself now defaults to colored output when used directly.

The change replaces raising ChunkedEncodingError on urllib3.ProtocolError with a bare pass inside the generator. This suppresses transport errors during streamed response iteration, causing truncated/broken responses to be treated as successful reads and potentially hanging or silently corrupting consumers. The previous behavior surfaced the error to callers, which is required for correctness.

The change updates internal imports in the vendored idna package to use relative imports, which is the correct fix for a packaged vendored copy. No functional logic changes were introduced, and the updated imports should prevent accidental resolution to any externally installed idna package.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect truncation / off-by-one leading to overlong short help output
verdict.rationale: The refactor changes truncation semantics and appears to introduce an edge-case bug for inputs whose first word alone exceeds max_length. In the truncation path, the code may end up returning only '...' even when a shorter prefix could be produced, because the backtracking loop can stop with i==0 and then slices with words[:i], dropping the first word entirely. More importantly, the new tests only assert `len(out) <= max_length + 3`, which would allow the previous bug to persist and does not verify the intended fix that output should never exceed `max_length`. The implementation also returns the full first sentence without checking its length when a period occurs, which can still exceed max_length. This makes the change risky for the advertised bugfix.

The condition in get_characters() was changed from an AND to an OR. This makes it yield almost every Unicode code point: any character that is a Python identifier continue char OR any character not matched by \w. Since most characters are not \w, the generated pattern will explode to include many invalid identifier characters. In addition, the generated file header lines now omit newline characters, concatenating the comment and the Python version comment with the import line, producing a malformed file.

The change only adds a standard Towncrier/Changelog notice for the upcoming release cycle and does not alter runtime code or existing release notes content. It appears to be documentation-only and acceptable.

The change relaxes the MarkupSafe requirement from >=0.23 to >=0, which removes the lower bound entirely. That can allow installation with very old MarkupSafe releases that may be incompatible with Jinja2 2.10.dev and break runtime behavior. Adding the Python 3.6 trove classifier is fine, but this dependency change introduces a compatibility defect.

The change only relaxes a timing assertion from > 0.0 to >= 0.0, which is harmless and avoids flakiness from near-zero elapsed times. The timeout test simply moves assertions inside the exception handler so they are guaranteed to reference the caught exception; this is a test correctness improvement, not a defect.

The patch changes Attribute.__init__ to swallow KeyError instead of raising TypeError for missing required constructor arguments. This means invalid Attribute construction now silently produces partially initialized objects, which can cause downstream attribute errors and breaks the class's invariant that all fields must be provided.
The change only updates release history and version/build constants for the 2.28.1 release. No functional code paths are modified, and no defect is introduced by these metadata/documentation updates.

Removing the explicit Pygments style setting is intentional for Furo compatibility and matches the referenced issue. Without a configured style, Sphinx/the theme can use its default styling, which is the desired behavior here. No functional defect is introduced by this change.

The change only standardizes capitalization from 'UNIX' to 'Unix' in comments, documentation, and one example docstring. It does not alter code logic, behavior, or public APIs, so no defect is introduced.

The change only adjusts the invalid-func error message formatting and relaxes the test to check the stable prefix rather than exact text. Behavior of matches_re is unchanged, and the new message still conveys the same information. No defect introduced.

The code change is consistent: select_autoescape now lowercases configured extensions and compares against the lowercased template suffix via endswith(), which makes matching case-insensitive as intended. The docs and tests were updated accordingly. No functional defect is apparent from the diff.

verdict.class_guess: syntax/logic regressions from whitespace refactor and accidental documentation edits
verdict.rationale: This change is not purely PEP8 cleanup; it introduces several functional defects. In requests.utils.header_expand, the indentation under the len(params) block is broken, so the comma separator is now nested incorrectly and can cause malformed header expansion. In decode_gzip, duplicate unreachable return lines were left, indicating accidental edit but not a runtime bug by itself. More importantly, requests.models._encode_params has been reformatted in a way that changes line structure around the append call and tuple return; while syntactically valid, it is easy to misread, but the bigger issue is that requests/config.py now contains arbitrary TODO text in the module docstring, which is harmless. The strongest concrete defect is the broken indentation in header_expand, which will affect header serialization and can break request headers.

The API refactor is internally inconsistent and breaks existing loader classes. In code, CachedLoaderMixin now inherits from object and its methods call super(CachedLoaderMixin, self).load(), which only works if a concrete loader base with load() appears after it in the MRO; this is a breaking change for any subclass not updated to use the new multiple-inheritance pattern. More critically, PackageLoader.get_source() still uses self.package (which is never defined; the instance stores package_name), and check_source_changed() calls resource_filename(name) with the wrong arguments. The new DictLoader also lacks its addition to __all__, so it is not exported by jinja.loaders despite documentation implying it is available from jinja import DictLoader. The docs snippet also shows a malformed import and constructor call, indicating the new API is not correctly documented/usable.

The patch changes the `test_http_put` method signature from `def test_http_put(self, mock_request):` to `def test_http_put(mock_request, self):`. This breaks unittest discovery/execution because the first argument should be `self`; as written, the test will receive the test instance in `mock_request` and the mock in `self`, causing attribute access and assertions to fail. Additionally, the test no longer has the correct parameter ordering for the `@mock.patch` decorator.

The moved test now asserts only one rendered line (`['42 = 23']`), but the template still renders two lines: one inside the `{% with %}` block and one after it. The original test in `test_ext.py` expected both `['42 = 23', '1 = 2']`. This change likely makes the test fail unless the second line is intentionally omitted by surrounding whitespace trimming, which the template does not do here.

The change only removes debug prints from one test and makes the timeout test more aggressive by reducing the timeout from 1e-7 to 1e-10. This is consistent with the PR description ('timeout adjustment') and does not introduce a functional defect in the code under review. The test should still behave as intended, though the stricter timeout may make the test slightly more brittle in slow environments.

The change only moves an async test fixture into conftest and adds new coverage for NativeEnvironment async rendering. The tests use the existing run_async_fn fixture correctly and the added assertions are consistent with the intended native-type behavior. No functional defect in the product code is introduced by this test-only change.

The changes are consistent with the apparent intent: parsing ordinals in dates is now more robust by stripping suffixes only when attached to a day number, and the release-date check now allows releases on the scheduled day while still rejecting future-dated releases. No obvious defect is introduced by these edits.

The change replaces direct access to __closure__ with getattr(..., None) to accommodate PyPy’s behavior when no closure exists. The resulting boolean check preserves the original intent (detect whether the dummy function created a closure) while avoiding an AttributeError on implementations that omit the attribute. No functional regression is evident from the diff.

The new logic replaces any callable object whose __call__ has a context/eval/environmentfunction marker with the bound __call__ method. Bound methods are not the same object as the underlying decorated function, so the later isinstance(__obj, _context_function_types) check is likely to fail and the context/eval/environment injection will never happen for callable instances. This breaks the intended feature for callable classes rather than enabling it.

The change mutates `resp.history` inside the redirect loop before the final history list is assembled, but `resp.history` is only updated for `i > 0` and is set to a shallow copy of an accumulating list. This produces incorrect/partial history semantics for intermediate responses and leaves the first redirect response with no history at all. More importantly, the generator later also builds `history` in `Session.send` and assigns it to the final response, so this added per-iteration mutation is redundant and can break invariants/tests expecting history to be finalized only on the last response. The test added is network-dependent and does not validate the actual history chain shape for each response robustly.

The change only updates documentation links and Sphinx linkcheck ignores. It does not alter runtime code or documented behavior, and the adjusted anchor case in the pyright URL is consistent with the existing reference target. No defect is introduced.

The change simply switches from inline open(...).read() calls to context-managed file reads, which closes the files properly and preserves the same values for long_description and license. No functional defect is introduced.

verdict.class_guess: async iteration detection / incorrect filter behavior for synchronous iterables
verdict.rationale: The async conversion now detects async iterables using `hasattr(value, '__anext__')` instead of `__aiter__`. This is incorrect for normal async iterables, which typically implement `__aiter__` but not `__anext__`, so they will fall through to the synchronous `for` loop and fail. The new `do_sum` also iterates with `auto_aiter(iterable)` without first normalizing non-iterable/async iterator inputs consistently, but the main defect is the broken async iterable check, which affects `list`, `join`, and any helper relying on `auto_to_seq`.

The change normalizes the empty-context path in get_choices() to return an empty list instead of None. This is safe because the caller iterates over the result, and an empty list preserves expected behavior while avoiding a TypeError. No new defect is introduced.

The change only updates the release status text in CHANGES.rst from 'Unreleased' to a specific release date for version 3.1.5. No code or behavioral logic is modified, so this does not introduce a defect.

The new loop aliases every already-imported top-level submodule under requests.packages, but it does not guarantee the parent package module objects are also preserved for nested imports in all cases. More importantly, it only runs once at import time, so any later-imported urllib3/idna/chardet submodules (or lazy imports triggered after this file executes) will not be mirrored into requests.packages, which can reintroduce identity mismatches and break the intended compatibility guarantee. The old explicit imports/aliases were simpler and more deterministic for the main package objects.

The change only updates a test to stop expecting a deprecated_call() warning that is no longer emitted, and removes an outdated pytest<8 pin because the test suite now passes with pytest 8+. The functional code path in assoc() is unchanged, and the modified test still verifies the intended exception behavior. No defect is introduced.

The new adapter is broken in multiple ways that will prevent HTTPS requests from working. In cert_verify(), it references undefined variables/attributes (`url`, `self.verify`, `self.cert`) instead of using the method parameters `verify` and `cert`. In send(), it calls `self._poolmanager` even though the attribute created in init_poolmanager() is `self.poolmanager`. As written, send() will raise an AttributeError before any request can be made.

The change narrows the environment marker for the win_inet_pton extra from python_version < '3.3' to an explicit equivalent set of versions (2.6 or 2.7). This preserves the intended behavior while avoiding range operators, and there is no functional regression apparent in the packaging metadata.

verdict.class_guess: slotted attrs classes with inherited cached_property methods
verdict.rationale: The new logic only appends cached_property functions to the closure-fixup list when `hasattr(self._cls, name)` is true. For inherited cached_properties, this is true, but the code deletes `cd[name]` from the new class dict before the later class creation. That means the cached_property descriptor is removed from the subclass, yet the inherited property remains on the base class. More importantly, the previous code updated closure cells for all cached_properties, whereas the new guard changes behavior for classes that define a cached_property only via inheritance or where the attribute lookup is affected by the just-mutated class dict. In slotted class construction, this can leave stale closure references or skip necessary updates for wrapped methods, causing incorrect `__class__`/`super()` behavior in cached_property accessors. The performance refactor is not behavior-preserving.

The change only removes an incorrect documentation claim and preserves the intended behavior by explicitly disabling match_args when auto_attribs is False. This matches the documented note that both attr.define and attr.s have match_args=True by default, while avoiding invalid match_args generation for non-auto-attribs classes.

The change replaces __attrs_attrs__ from a plain tuple to a tuple subclass with named properties. Existing tuple behavior is preserved, and the added tests cover property access for fields(). I don’t see a functional regression in the modified code path.

The new test server can fail in `run()` before `sock` is assigned (e.g. bind/listen errors). The `finally` block then unconditionally references `sock.close()`, which raises `UnboundLocalError` and masks the original failure. In addition, `text_response_server()` instantiates `Server(...)` instead of `cls(...)`, so subclasses calling this classmethod will get the base `Server` type rather than the subclass, breaking extensibility. The first issue can cause test crashes and leaked error context; the second is a functional regression in the helper API.
The change only adds a trailing comma after the final entry in the FILTERS dictionary, which fixes the syntax error and does not alter runtime behavior.

verdict.class_guess: Regression test incorrectly updated to expect a generator path and changed the regression reference
verdict.rationale: The test was originally a regression test for #1417 and asserted that a generator-based field_transformer still produces the expected fields. The update changes the wording to refer to #1416 and weakens the assertion to filter out unnamed attributes, which makes the test less precise and appears to misattribute the regression. This could let a real regression in generator-based field_transformer handling slip by unnoticed.

The old code joined path segments with the OS separator, producing a single relative path under searchpath. The new code passes the segments as separate arguments to os.path.join, which changes semantics: if a template name contains absolute-path-like pieces or leading separators, path.join can reset to those components. More importantly, the filtering only removes segments starting with '.', so names like 'foo//bar' or 'foo/..' are handled differently than before, and the test added does not cover the broken case. This change also makes the function dependent on path.join's variadic behavior, but its main effect is altered template path resolution and potential directory traversal / incorrect lookup.

The change to do_slice() alters the computed end index from an exclusive Python slice bound to an inclusive-looking bound by subtracting 1. Since seq[start:end] excludes end, reducing it by 1 drops the last item from each produced slice and can also shorten slices inconsistently. This is a functional bug introduced by the patch. The attr docstring change is fine.

The new PreparedRequest.prepare_url() omits the requote_uri() step that the existing Request.full_url property performs. That means paths and query strings containing reserved or non-ASCII characters will no longer be percent-encoded before sending, which can produce invalid URLs or behavior changes versus the previous implementation. In addition, Request.prepare() now only calls prepare_method() and prepare_url(), leaving headers/data/cookies/auth/etc. unprepared and effectively making the new request path incomplete/broken if used.

The runtime changes are not documentation-only and introduce behavior regressions. In Context.__getitem__, resolving via self.resolve() and then checking isinstance(item, Undefined) breaks environments that use a custom Undefined subclass not imported in this scope and changes lookup semantics; it also makes missing-variable detection depend on class identity rather than the existing key lookup flow. In LoopContext.length, replacing tuple materialization with list(iterable[self.index0 + 1:]) and setting _next to iter(...).next skips already-consumed items inconsistently and can produce wrong iteration behavior when length is computed mid-iteration, especially for generators. The TemplateModule.__unicode__ change removes explicit unicode() coercion, which can alter return types/encoding expectations for subclasses or concat implementations. These are not safe for a doc fix.

verdict.class_guess: compatibility regression in Python version gating
verdict.rationale: The version check was changed from `sys.version_info >= (3, 0)` to `sys.version_info > (3, 0)`, which excludes Python 3.0 exactly. That means the `use_2to3` path will no longer be enabled on Python 3.0, changing packaging behavior for that interpreter and likely breaking installation/runtime compatibility there.

The refactor changes buffering semantics in a way that can deadlock or stop early. The old implementation buffered until it had collected `size` non-empty chunks, but it called `get_next(self._gen)` once per buffered generator creation, so the buffered iterator advanced correctly. The new `_buffered_generator` is an iterator, but `enable_buffering` wraps it with `partial(next, self._buffered_generator(size))`, creating a fresh generator object only once per buffering enable. However, inside `_buffered_generator`, `next(self._gen)` is called directly, which is fine, but the change from `size <= 1` to `size < 1` alters the public contract and allows `size=1` buffering, which now changes behavior from raising to buffering a single item. More importantly, `disable_buffering` now uses `partial(next, self._gen)` instead of the previous `get_next`, which is equivalent, but the rewritten generator logic no longer matches the prior nested-generator design and relies on truthiness of chunks for size accounting; this can skip flushing when the stream ends after falsy chunks and may yield empty output unexpectedly. The most concrete regression is that the buffered generator no longer consumes from the wrapped iterator through the same callable abstraction, making the implementation harder to reason about and prone to incorrect StopIteration handling at stream boundaries.

The patch only updates documentation, changelog text, and the expected doctest output to match the existing validator behavior of including the attribute, options, and value in the ValueError args. The small test formatting change is non-functional. No code-path defect is introduced by this change.

The change adds explicit pickle support for Response by serializing a curated set of attributes and restoring them on unpickle. This is consistent with the stated goal and the test covers basic round-trip behavior. The main caveat is that only a subset of attributes is preserved, but that appears intentional and does not introduce an immediate defect in the modified code path.

The change only adds a changelog entry and adjusts a typing example to reflect the new TypeGuard behavior. The new example still exercises the intended narrowing and does not appear to introduce runtime or typing defects. The combined `attrs.has(foo) and attr.has(foo)` check is acceptable because both functions should narrow `foo` to an attrs instance.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect __all__ population causes exported names to break
verdict.rationale: The change replaces `__all__.extend((define, field, frozen, mutable))` with string names. `__all__` should contain exported symbol names, so using strings is correct; however the previous code was actually invalid because it attempted to extend with function objects, which would break consumers expecting string names and could also affect tooling. The only potential defect in this diff is the version bump to a dev release and changelog note, which are acceptable for start of cycle. No new runtime defect is introduced by the shown patch.

The new Travis script runs `tox -e py` unconditionally, but `tox.ini` defines only version-specific environments (`py26`, `py27`, `pypy`, `py33`, `py34`, `py35`) and no generic `py` environment. This will make Travis fail to find the requested tox env. The previous script computed the matching env from `TRAVIS_PYTHON_VERSION`, so the change breaks CI execution.

verdict.class_guess: logic bug in bash completion context resolution / chained-command completion
verdict.rationale: The new resolve_ctx loop can walk past the terminal chained subcommand context and return its parent MultiCommand context instead of the final parsed subcommand. Because the completion logic later uses ctx.command and ctx.params, this can make completions come from the wrong command for chained invocations. In addition, the new chained-command branch computes remaining_commands from the parent MultiCommand minus ctx.parent.protected_args; this set difference is based on parsed option tokens, not the already-consumed chained subcommands, so it can re-offer commands that were already used in the chain or miss the correct remaining command set. The indentation in the new loop also suggests the parsing behavior changed materially rather than just extending it.

The new condition references `super_cls`, but `super_cls` is a list of inherited `Attribute` objects, not a membership set of names or attributes in the current class. As written, `a not in super_cls` will skip setting any attribute that compares equal to an inherited attribute object, which is not the intended test for 'own attributes only'. More importantly, when `these is None`, `a` is an `Attribute` object from `cl.__attrs_attrs__`; inherited and local attributes are all mixed into that list, and equality semantics can cause incorrect skipping or setting depending on attribute contents. This change is likely to break attribute assignment for inherited classes and does not correctly determine whether an attribute belongs to the superclass or the class itself.

The __lt__ method parameters were swapped to (other, self), which breaks the instance method contract. Python will still bind the instance as the first argument, so inside the method 'other' is actually self and 'self' is the compared object. The call to self._ensure_tuple(other) then uses the wrong object and will typically fail or compare incorrectly, breaking VersionInfo ordering.

The removed test is redundant with test_mixed_case_scheme_acceptable, which already exercises an uppercase HTTP scheme ('HTTP://') along with other mixed-case variants. No behavioral code change is introduced, so this deletion does not appear to add a defect.

The new test only exercises an existing hidden-progressbar code path by forcing isatty() to return False and asserting no output is produced. It does not appear to introduce a product defect. The only other diff hunk changes an assertion in test_secho from bytes == b'' to bytes != b'', which seems unrelated but is in the test suite and not a defect in application code.

The change only adds the Sphinx not-found extension to the docs configuration and declares its dependency in the docs extra. This is consistent and should not affect runtime behavior. No defect is apparent from the diff.

verdict.class_guess: Incorrect boolean logic in simple_class helper breaks the intended eq=True behavior and the moved test module may still not be collected depending on the test runner's discovery rules.
verdict.rationale: The change alters `simple_class` from `eq=eq or order` to `eq=eq and order`, which changes semantics: calling `simple_class(eq=True, order=False)` will now produce a class with `eq=False`, unlike before. This is a behavioral regression in a shared test helper and can invalidate existing tests. Additionally, the relocated test file `tests/test_utils.py` may or may not be picked up depending on how the suite is configured, but the logic bug alone is a clear defect.

The change consistently adds a hidden flag to Command and Option, and help rendering now skips hidden subcommands and options. Hidden subcommands also naturally hide their descendants because the parent group’s command listing omits them. The added tests cover the intended behavior. No obvious regressions or API conflicts are introduced by this diff.

verdict.class_guess: behavioral regression in Option help rendering
verdict.rationale: The new global `Context.show_default` flag is checked correctly, but the help text formatting still tests `self.show_default` to decide whether to use a custom string. If an option is created with `show_default=False` and the context has `show_default=True`, the code will enter the block and then evaluate `isinstance(self.show_default, string_types)`, which is false, so custom string defaults are not supported by the global override. More importantly, the intended precedence comment says the command-level setting overrides option-level settings, but this implementation only forces visibility; it does not preserve the option-level string semantics and can produce incorrect/default formatting for options that rely on string-valued `show_default`.

The change only updates setup metadata to explicitly mark Python 3.4 as unsupported and makes a small internal refactor of read() that does not alter behavior. The python_requires string now matches the stated compatibility break, and the path temporary variable is functionally equivalent.

The new Tidelift URL is split across two adjacent string literals, but the first line is missing a closing quote/concatenation separator in a way that makes the dict entry syntactically invalid as shown. Even if intended as implicit string concatenation, the formatting is broken and risks a parse error. Separately, the read() helper now calls codecs.open(path, ...) after converting absolute paths to relative paths, which changes behavior and can break file reads when the path is already absolute or when called from a different working directory. This is a functional regression in metadata generation.

The change preserves behavior while avoiding per-call set allocation by using tuple literals for membership checks. The only functional change is improved error formatting with !r, which makes the exception message more precise and matches the updated docs. No defect is apparent.

The change correctly distinguishes between stderr not being captured (None) and an actually captured but empty stderr buffer (empty bytes), which fixes the reported ValueError behavior. The updated test covers the empty-stderr case, and the new implementation in CliRunner.invoke sets stderr=None only when mix_stderr=True. No regression is apparent from the diff.

The patch flips the reload staleness checks in every loader from "changed means reload" to "unchanged means reload". In BaseLoader, FileSystemLoader, and PackageLoader this causes uptodate() to return True when the template file has not changed and False when it has, which inverts auto-reload behavior. Templates will be recompiled unnecessarily and, more importantly, modified templates will be incorrectly treated as up-to-date and not reloaded.

The patch tightens sandbox attribute access by blocking additional introspection paths on classes, code objects, tracebacks, frames, and generator frames. The added type checks and the undefined() call change appear consistent with the existing sandbox design and do not introduce an obvious functional regression in the shown code. The behavior change is security-motivated and acceptable based on the diff provided.

The change adds a __str__ implementation for MissingParameter so it can be safely string-cast in interactive Python, while preserving the existing formatted error output via format_message(). The new tests cover both argument and option cases. No functional regression is apparent from the diff.

The loop test now skips `reversed(slist)`, so it no longer verifies `loop` behavior for reversed iterables. More importantly, `TestWith.test_with` was changed to use the shared `env` fixture instead of constructing an `Environment(extensions=['jinja2.ext.with_'])`; if the shared fixture does not enable the `with_` extension, this test will fail or no longer actually exercise the feature under test. This is a behavior regression in the test suite rather than an optimization.
